content:
## Physical activity: For health professionals
- Being physically active for your health
- Supporting healthy communities
- Safe practices for physical activity
- For health professionals
- Physical activity in Canada
- Surveillance and research

## Physical activity in Canada
Physical  inactivity and sedentary behaviour levels remain high in Canada.
Physical inactivity is an important public health concern for  people of all ages. The World Health Organization identifies physical  inactivity as the fourth leading risk factor for global mortality. It is also  linked to several chronic diseases, including:
- cardiovascular  disease
It  is recommended that children and youth engage in moderate-to-vigorous physical  activity for at least 60 minutes each day.
For  adults, the recommendation is 150 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical  activity each week.
Less than  half of adults, children and youth in Canada meet the recommended guidelines.
Males  are more likely to meet the guidelines than females in every age group.
Young  adults aged 18 to 34 years are most likely to be active. This rate  declines with age and is the lowest among those aged 65 years and older in  Canada.
- Canada's 24-Hour Movement Guidelines (Canadian  Society for Exercise Physiology)
Health  inequalities refer to differences in health status experienced by different  groups in society. This is related to physical activity because physical  activity levels vary between and within different population groups in Canada.
Lower  rates of physical activity are especially pronounced for:
- Indigenous  populations
- those  with poor self-rated general health
- those  who smoke or have abdominal obesity
- racialized,  immigrant and newcomer populations
- households  with lower education and income levels
- children,  youth and older adults, especially those with longer-term health conditions or  disabilities
Common  barriers to being physically active include time, family commitments and  fatigue. In addition, social and economic factors impact people’s ability to  engage in physical activity.
Health  experts recognize that our ability to change our behaviour and health habits,  including being physically active, is closely tied to several social  determinants of health.
Social  determinants of heath are interrelated social, political and economic circumstances  in which people are born, grow up, live, work and age. We can influence some of  these factors, but not all. Factors can include:
- family       arrangement
- age, sex       and gender
- income and       education
- culture       and social supports
- health       status and chronic conditions
- employment       and working conditions
- Health inequalities data tool
- Social determinants of health and health inequalities
- Health Behaviour  in School-aged Children study in Canada
- Understanding the Report on Key Health Inequalities in  Canada
- Inequalities in mental health, well-being  and wellness in Canada
- Inventory of Pan-Canadian Health Inequalities Reporting  Initiative
- Intersecting risk factors for physical inactivity among  Canadian adults

### COVID-19  pandemic and physical activity
The COVID-19 pandemic greatly  impacted the physical activity habits of people in Canada. Certain population  groups were disproportionately affected by pandemic restrictions and closures,  including:
- people  with lower incomes
- people  without access to safe and accessible outdoor spaces
As new data becomes available, we  will be able to better understand long-term impacts of the pandemic on physical  activity levels and leverage these data for future programming.
- Youth (but not adults)  reported less physical activity during the COVID-19 pandemic
- Unequal impact of the  COVID-19 pandemic on the physical activity habits of Canadians
- Ongoing impact of the  COVID-19 pandemic on physical activity and screen time among Canadian adults
- Ongoing impact of the  COVID-19 pandemic on physical activity and screen time among Canadian youth

### Persons  with disabilities and physical activity
More than 1 in 4 Canadians aged 15  years and over have a disability, including physical, mental, intellectual,  genetic, learning, communication or sensory impairments. People living with  disabilities are disproportionately affected by barriers to engage in physical  activity. Some of the most common barriers can include:
- built  environment features, including accessibility
Barriers can vary depending on the  type of disability. Types of disability can include physical or motor, sensory,  intellectual or psychosocial.
Physical activity plays an  important role in maintaining health, well-being and quality of life for  everyone, including persons with disabilities. Persons with disabilities should  consult a health professional to determine what types of physical activity are  most suitable and safe.
- Canada’s Disability  Inclusion and Action Plan
- Equity, diversity,  inclusion and participation in sport
- Key  health inequalities in Canada: Executive summary
- Demographic,  employment and income profile of persons with disabilities

## Surveillance and research
The  Government of Canada monitors physical activity levels in Canada in various  ways.

#### Physical  activity, sedentary behaviour and sleep indicators
The physical activity, sedentary behaviour  and sleep (PASS) indicators provide important surveillance information on  physical activity, sedentary and sleep behaviours for people in Canada and  factors that influence them.
The PASS indicators include unique  indicators and their measures grouped by movement behaviour (24-hour movement,  physical activity, sedentary behaviour and sleep) and 3 key domains:
- built/society environment
- family and social environment
- Physical activity, sedentary behaviour  and sleep (PASS) indicators

#### Canadian  Risk Factor Atlas
The  Canadian Risk Factor Atlas (CRFA) is an interactive database that provides  insights into the prevalence of key chronic disease risk factors across Canada.  It presents data through maps and graphs, allowing users to explore age- and  sex-specific trends by sociodemographic characteristics and geographic regions.
The CRFA  uses a common risk factor approach to monitor changes in major chronic disease risk factors, including  physical inactivity, recreational screen time, sedentary transportation,  smoking, heavy alcohol consumption, as well as obesity.
The CRFA  presents data in the form of maps and trends overtime.
- CRFA: Long-term  risk factor trends
- CRFA: COVID-19  pandemic risk factor trends
- CRFA: Maps  (geographic regions) of risk factors

#### Canadian  Community Health Survey
The Canadian Community Health  Survey collects self-reported health information annually, including  information on:
- health  care utilization
- health  determinants for the Canadian population
- Canadian  Community Health Survey

#### Canadian  Health Measures Survey
The Canadian Health Measures Survey  is a comprehensive health survey conducted in Canada. It collects information  through interviews and direct measures, including the use of activity monitors  to collect information on physical activity.
- Canadian Health  Measures Survey
- Canadian  Health Measures Survey: Direct health measures interactive dashboard
The  Government of Canada conducts a wide range of research on physical activity and  related areas to generate knowledge, inform public health action, advance  evidence-based policy and practice across Canada.
Physical  activity has many intersections with other topics, including:
- climate change and  environment
- safety, accessibility and health  equity
- sports, injury prevention  and concussion
- participation, inclusion  and mental health
- built environment and  active transportation
- health promotion and  chronic disease prevention
- physical activity in different settings, like schools  and even outer space
Advancing  research on physical activity requires interdisciplinary approaches and  collaboration between:
- community       groups
- health       professionals
- scientists       and researchers
Canadian  Institute of Health Research
- Institutes of Population and Public Health
- Institute of Musculoskeletal Health and Arthritis
Canadian  Space Agency

Public  Health Agency of Canada

### Canada’s 24-Hour Movement Guidelines
With funding from the Government of Canada, the Canadian  Society for Exercise Physiology, together with other experts and stakeholders,  developed movement guidelines for Canadians of all ages. These guidelines cover  the optimal amounts of physical activity, sedentary behaviour and sleep, as  well as the best combination of these behaviours. Known as Canada’s 24-Hour  Movement Guidelines, they provide clear guidance on what a healthy day looks  like for people in Canada. The guidelines include:
- age-specific  recommendations for optimal amounts of physical activity, sedentary behaviour and sleep
- recommendations for different intensities of  physical activity, and the inclusion of muscle strengthening and balance  activities, including: light-intensity physical activity, which are activities  that do not result in a substantial increase in heart rate or breathing examples include tai chi, stretching, and standing  while going light tasks moderate  to vigorous physical activity that will raise your heart rate and make you  breathe faster examples include hiking, skating, dancing, playing  sports like basketball or soccer muscle and bone  strengthening activity, which are activities and exercises that increase  skeletal muscle and bone strength, power, endurance and mass examples include yoga,  climbing stairs, lifting weights, walking on uneven ground, bodyweight  exercises like push-ups
- light-intensity physical activity, which are activities  that do not result in a substantial increase in heart rate or breathing examples include tai chi, stretching, and standing  while going light tasks
- examples include tai chi, stretching, and standing  while going light tasks
- moderate  to vigorous physical activity that will raise your heart rate and make you  breathe faster examples include hiking, skating, dancing, playing  sports like basketball or soccer
- examples include hiking, skating, dancing, playing  sports like basketball or soccer
- muscle and bone  strengthening activity, which are activities and exercises that increase  skeletal muscle and bone strength, power, endurance and mass examples include yoga,  climbing stairs, lifting weights, walking on uneven ground, bodyweight  exercises like push-ups
- examples include yoga,  climbing stairs, lifting weights, walking on uneven ground, bodyweight  exercises like push-ups
- acknowledgement that some physical activity is better  than none and increased physical activity of any kind can lead to greater  health benefits
Meeting the recommended physical activity, sedentary  behaviour and sleep levels is key to:
- preventing  chronic disease
- living  longer and healthier lives
- improving  physical and mental health
For  example, adults who met all 24-Hour Movement Guidelines for physical activity,  sedentary behaviour and sleep levels had more favourable body mass index, waist  circumference, aerobic fitness scores (cardiovascular and endurance fitness that  is promoted through activities like running, walking, cycling and swimming).
- Are Canadian adults getting enough sleep?  (infographic)
- Are Canadian children getting enough sleep?  (infographic)
- Canada's 24-Hour Movement Guidelines (Canada  Society for Exercise Physiology) The Whole Day Matters Toolkit for Primary Care The Whole Day Matters Toolkit for Physiotherapists
- The Whole Day Matters Toolkit for Primary Care
- The Whole Day Matters Toolkit for Physiotherapists

### International movement guidelines
The  World Health Organization (WHO) provides global leadership on physical activity  and is responsible for developing evidence-based guidelines and recommendations  on effective policy actions.
These  guidelines provides evidence-based public health  recommendations for:
- pregnancy  and post-partum
- people  living with chronic conditions and disabilities
The  recommendations detail the amount of physical activity (frequency, intensity  and duration) required to offer significant health benefits and to reduce  health risks. The guidelines highlight that:
- any  amount of physical activity is better than none
- all  physical activity counts
- all age  groups should limit sedentary time
- muscle  strengthening benefits everyone
WHO Global Guidelines on Physical Activity  and Sedentary Behaviour
In  addition to global guidelines, many countries have adopted their own physical  activity guidelines.
The  Global Action Plan on Physical Activity 2018 to 2030 is a global roadmap developed by the WHO to  increase physical activity and reduce sedentary behaviours through 20  evidence-based policy actions across 4 areas.
WHO Global Action Plan on Physical Activity  2018 to 2030
By  monitoring physical activity behaviours and policy implementation, the WHO  tracks progress using their guidelines on physical activity and sedentary  behaviour towards achieving the target of a 15% relative reduction in the  global prevalence of physical inactivity in adolescents and adults by 2030.
Other  resources from the WHO include:
- Fact sheet on physical activity
- Global Levels of Physical Inactivity in Adults: Off Track  for 2030
- Global Status Report on Physical  Activity 2022: Let's Get Moving
